message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
This is a dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (do NOT flag as bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only, nothing leaves the box), no metered API (subscription OAuth only), engine never imports adapters/datasets (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends are lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; prefer findings you can back with a concrete repro/PoC against the venv. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects (not style/nits). Each finding needs a real file path and, where possible, a line.

You are independent VERIFIER #2 of 2. A prior adversarial assessor reported this production-readiness finding:

TITLE: BaselineArtifact deep-freezes its entire contents element-by-element, turning a similarity matrix into a recursively tuple-ized copy
DIMENSION: performance & scale
CLAIMED SEVERITY: HIGH
FILE: src/assay_engine/_frozen.py 61-102 (freeze); src/assay_engine/baseline/toolkit.py:38-40
DESCRIPTION: BaselineArtifact.__post_init__ runs freeze_mapping over `contents`, and freeze() recurses through every list/tuple converting it element-by-element into nested tuples (lines 75-76) and hashes every leaf. A study that puts its similarity matrix / embedding matrix (the natural baseline contents) into `contents` as nested Python lists pays a second full O(n_cells) traversal that allocates a parallel nested-tuple copy AND calls hash() on every float. PoC against the venv: freezing a 2000x2000 (4M-cell) matrix into the artifact took 3.29 s and 64 MB on top of the original. Combined with the previous finding, a 5k-embedding baseline that builds an in-memory similarity matrix is materialized at least three times (numpy array -> nested list -> nested tuple). Breaking point: ~3-5k units with any dense O(n^2) content; for raw embedding matrices (n x d) it scales with n*d and a 100k-doc x 768-dim embedding set (77M cells) is hundreds of MB-to-GB of duplicated tuples plus tens of seconds of pure-Python work at artifact construction.
SUGGESTED FIX: Do not deep-freeze large numeric payloads cell-by-cell. The freeze() already has array-like handling (lines 85-94) that canonicalizes objects exposing tobytes()/shape to a single (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor in O(1) wrappers — steer builders/contracts toward numpy/array.array for bulk numeric content so this fast path is taken, and/or add a shallow-immutable container for large arrays so contents are not recursively tuple-ized. At minimum, document that `contents` must hold buffer-backed arrays (not nested Python lists) for any payload above a few thousand cells.

Independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the actual code; attempt a concrete PoC against the venv if applicable. Set is_real=false if it is a false positive, already-handled, or merely a documented/justified design choice given the hard constraints. Recalibrate severity. Provide your evidence (what you read/ran).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Deep-immutable, hashable mapping for the engine's frozen value records.
2	
3	Many engine dataclasses are ``frozen=True`` and carry mapping fields (``evidence``,
4	``metadata``, ``attributes``, ``params``, ``assertion``, ``provenance``, ``contents``,
5	``determinism``). With a plain ``dict`` default two problems arise (audit pass 1, issue #7):
6	
7	1. ``frozen=True`` auto-generates ``__hash__`` over all fields, so the instances *look*
8	   hashable but raise ``TypeError`` the moment they are hashed (a dict is unhashable).
9	2. ``frozen`` only blocks attribute rebinding; the contained dict stays mutable in place,
10	   so a "frozen" provenance record can be silently altered after construction.
11	
12	:class:`FrozenDict` fixes both: it is an immutable :class:`~collections.abc.Mapping` and is
13	hashable. :func:`freeze` recursively converts nested mappings/sequences/sets so the whole
14	structure is immutable and hashable. Each affected dataclass normalizes its mapping fields
15	through :func:`freeze` in ``__post_init__`` (via ``object.__setattr__`` to satisfy frozen).
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from collections.abc import Mapping
21	from typing import Any, Iterator
22	
23	
24	class FrozenDict(Mapping[str, Any]):
25	    """An immutable, hashable mapping. Item assignment is impossible; hashing is stable."""
26	
27	    __slots__ = ("_data", "_hash")
28	
29	    def __init__(self, data: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None) -> None:
30	        # Freeze values so a directly-constructed FrozenDict satisfies its own
31	        # immutable+hashable contract even from nested-mutable input (audit pass 3, issue #27).
32	        # freeze() is idempotent, so routing through freeze_mapping costs only one extra pass.
33	        self._data: dict[str, Any] = (
34	            {k: freeze(v) for k, v in data.items()} if data is not None else {}
35	        )
36	        self._hash: int | None = None
37	
38	    def __getitem__(self, key: str) -> Any:
39	        return self._data[key]
40	
41	    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[str]:
42	        return iter(self._data)
43	
44	    def __len__(self) -> int:
45	        return len(self._data)
46	
47	    def __repr__(self) -> str:
48	        return f"FrozenDict({self._data!r})"
49	
50	    def __hash__(self) -> int:
51	        if self._hash is None:
52	            self._hash = hash(frozenset(self._data.items()))
53	        return self._hash
54	
55	    def __eq__(self, other: object) -> bool:
56	        if isinstance(other, Mapping):
57	            return dict(self._data) == dict(other)
58	        return NotImplemented
59	
60	
61	def freeze(value: Any) -> Any:
62	    """Recursively convert ``value`` into an immutable, hashable form.
63	
64	    Mappings → :class:`FrozenDict`, lists/tuples → tuples, sets → frozensets. Known mutable
65	    buffers are converted to hashable equivalents (``bytearray`` → ``bytes``;
66	    ``array.array`` / numpy-like arrays exposing ``tobytes`` → a ``(kind, shape, bytes)``
67	    tuple). Any remaining leaf that is not hashable raises ``TypeError`` *here, at
68	    construction time* — rather than silently passing through and resurfacing the error the
69	    moment the record is hashed (audit pass 2, issue #19; the incomplete half of #7).
70	    """
71	    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
72	        return value
73	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
74	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
75	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
76	        return tuple(freeze(v) for v in value)
77	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
78	        return frozenset(freeze(v) for v in value)
79	    if isinstance(value, bytearray):
80	        return bytes(value)
81	    # Duck-typed buffer/array support (e.g. array.array, numpy.ndarray) without importing
82	    # numpy: an array-like exposing tobytes() is canonicalized to a hashable descriptor tuple.
83	    # Require an array-ish marker (shape/typecode/dtype) so an unrelated object that merely
84	    # happens to have a tobytes() is left to the hashability check below (fix-review nit, #19).
85	    tobytes = getattr(value, "tobytes", None)
86	    array_like = hasattr(value, "shape") or hasattr(value, "typecode") or hasattr(value, "dtype")
87	    if callable(tobytes) and array_like and not isinstance(value, (bytes, str)):
88	        shape = getattr(value, "shape", None)
89	        kind = getattr(getattr(value, "dtype", None), "str", None) or getattr(
90	            value, "typecode", type(value).__name__
91	        )
92	        # Route the descriptor through freeze() so a malformed tobytes() returning a
93	        # non-hashable value is still caught loud at construction (audit pass 3, issue #26).
94	        return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape) if shape is not None else None, tobytes()))
95	    try:
96	        hash(value)
97	    except TypeError as exc:
98	        raise TypeError(
99	            f"freeze: leaf of type {type(value).__name__!r} is not hashable and has no "
100	            "known immutable conversion; a frozen value record cannot contain it"
101	        ) from exc
102	    return value
103	
104	
105	def freeze_mapping(value: Mapping[str, Any]) -> FrozenDict:
106	    """Normalize a mapping field to a deep-immutable :class:`FrozenDict`."""
107	    return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
108

1	"""Baseline builder contract + a deterministic artifact wrapper.
2	
3	This module fixes the *shape* of a baseline artifact and the build contract. The engine ships
4	the dataset-agnostic building blocks for a baseline (similarity/distance, descriptive stats,
5	the determinism harness in :mod:`assay_engine.baseline`); the choice-bearing builder that
6	assembles them — which embedding model, which clustering, which graph — encodes dataset
7	decisions and is supplied by a study's adapter as a :class:`BaselineBuilder` (ADR-0002).
8	
9	Firewall A is enforced here structurally: :meth:`BaselineBuilder.build` accepts a
10	:class:`~assay_engine.methodology.firewalls.ClaimBlindGuard` and never accepts a claims
11	source — a baseline simply cannot be handed the claims it must stay blind to.
12	"""
13	
14	from __future__ import annotations
15	
16	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
17	from typing import Any, Mapping, Protocol
18	
19	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
20	from assay_engine.contracts.claims import ExternalClaimsSource
21	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
22	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import ClaimBlindGuard
23	
24	
25	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
26	class BaselineArtifact:
27	    """A deterministic, versioned empirical model of the corpus — the one privileged object.
28	
29	    Concrete contents (embedding matrices, similarity graphs, cluster assignments, etc.) live
30	    under ``contents`` keyed by builder name. ``determinism`` records the seeds, input hashes,
31	    and model/version strings needed to reproduce it.
32	    """
33	
34	    corpus_fingerprint: str
35	    contents: Mapping[str, Any]
36	    determinism: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
37	
38	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
39	        object.__setattr__(self, "contents", freeze_mapping(self.contents))
40	        object.__setattr__(self, "determinism", freeze_mapping(self.determinism))
41	
42	
43	class BaselineBuilder(Protocol):
44	    """Build a :class:`BaselineArtifact` from a corpus, blind to any external claims."""
45	
46	    def build(
47	        self, corpus: Corpus, *, claim_guard: ClaimBlindGuard[ExternalClaimsSource]
48	    ) -> BaselineArtifact:
49	        """Construct the baseline. Must run with ``claim_guard.sealed()`` in force."""
50	        ...
51

content.text: The code confirms the mechanics. Let me run a PoC against the venv to verify the claimed performance impact with nested Python lists vs numpy arrays.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
import time, sys, tracemalloc
from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact

# Case 1: nested Python lists (the 'natural' contents per finding)
n = 2000
mat = [[float(i*j % 7) for j in range(n)] for i in range(n)]
print('built list matrix', n, 'x', n)

tracemalloc.start()
t0 = time.perf_counter()
art = BaselineArtifact(corpus_fingerprint='x', contents={'sim': mat})
t1 = time.perf_counter()
cur, peak = tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()
tracemalloc.stop()
print('nested-list freeze: %.3f s, peak +%.1f MB' % (t1-t0, peak/1e6))
print('frozen type:', type(art.contents['sim']))
"

nested-list freeze: 3.601 s, peak +64.3 MB
frozen type: <class 'tuple'>

content.text: Confirmed: 2000x2000 nested list = 3.6s and +64MB. Now let me check the numpy fast path and whether the engine's own building blocks produce numpy arrays (so the natural path avoids this).

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
import time, tracemalloc
import numpy as np
from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact

n = 2000
mat = np.arange(n*n, dtype=np.float64).reshape(n,n)
tracemalloc.start()
t0 = time.perf_counter()
art = BaselineArtifact(corpus_fingerprint='x', contents={'sim': mat})
t1 = time.perf_counter()
cur, peak = tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()
tracemalloc.stop()
print('numpy freeze: %.4f s, peak +%.2f MB' % (t1-t0, peak/1e6))
print('frozen repr head:', repr(art.contents['sim'])[:80])
"

content.text: The numpy fast path is dramatically faster (0.0075s vs 3.6s, ~480x) and the +32MB is just `tobytes()` (a single copy, unavoidable for hashing). Now let me check what the engine's own baseline building blocks actually produce — does the engine steer toward numpy or nested lists?

content.text: This is the key finding. The engine's own `cosine_similarity_matrix` returns `list[list[float]]` (lines 115, 112, 86), NOT a numpy array. So the engine's primary baseline-building block produces exactly the nested-list structure that triggers the slow path.

content.content:
115	def cosine_similarity_matrix(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
116	    """Full symmetric cosine-similarity matrix (diagonal 1.0 for non-zero vectors, 0.0 for zero).
117	
118	    Pure-Python is O(n²·d) and does not scale (a corpus of thousands of embeddings would take
119	    minutes-to-hours); when numpy is available (the optional ``baseline`` extra) this dispatches
120	    to a vectorized path that is orders of magnitude faster. The two paths agree to within
121	    floating-point tolerance (both clamp to [-1, 1]); a study needing byte-identical
122	    reproducibility should pin its environment (the determinism harness records component
123	    versions for exactly this reason).
124	    """
125	    if not rows:
126	        return []
127	    try:
128	        import numpy  # noqa: F401
129	    except ImportError:
130	        return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows)
131	    return _cosine_matrix_numpy(rows)
132	
133	
134	def _quantile(sorted_values: list[float], q: float) -> float:
135	    """Linear-interpolation quantile (``q`` in [0, 1]) over already-sorted values."""
136	    if not 0.0 <= q <= 1.0:
137	        raise ValueError(f"quantile q must be in [0, 1]; got {q}")
138	    n = len(sorted_values)
139	    if n == 1:
140	        return sorted_values[0]
141	    pos = q * (n - 1)
142	    lo = math.floor(pos)
143	    hi = math.ceil(pos)
144	    if lo == hi:
145	        return sorted_values[lo]
146	    frac = pos - lo
147	    return sorted_values[lo] * (1 - frac) + sorted_values[hi] * frac
148	
149

content.text:
This confirms a real tension: the engine's own primitive `cosine_similarity_matrix` returns `list[list[float]]`, which is the natural thing a builder would put into `contents`, and that hits the slow O(n²) tuple-ization path. Even `_cosine_matrix_numpy` converts back to nested lists (line 112) before returning.

Let me verify the realistic breaking point and check whether there's any guidance/documentation steering builders to numpy.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rniE "buffer-backed|tobytes|nested list|numpy|array.array|contents must|tolist" docs/ src/assay_engine/baseline/toolkit.py 2>/dev/null | grep -iv "import numpy" | head -30

cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
import time, tracemalloc
from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact

# Realistic: 5000 embeddings -> similarity matrix as nested list (engine primitive output)
n = 5000
mat = [[0.0]*n for _ in range(n)]  # already-allocated, just freeze cost
tracemalloc.start()
t0 = time.perf_counter()
art = BaselineArtifact(corpus_fingerprint='x', contents={'sim': mat})
t1 = time.perf_counter()
cur, peak = tracemalloc.get_traced_memory()
tracemalloc.stop()
print('5k x 5k (25M cells) nested-list freeze: %.2f s, peak +%.0f MB' % (t1-t0, peak/1e6))
"

content.content: 5k x 5k (25M cells) nested-list freeze: 21.15 s, peak +401 MB

content.text:
Confirmed. A 5000-embedding similarity matrix (the natural output of the engine's own `cosine_similarity_matrix`) takes 21 seconds and +401MB just to freeze at artifact construction. The numbers in the finding are accurate and reproducible.

Let me verify the hash cost claim too (the hash on the frozen tuple), since `BaselineArtifact` is `frozen=True` and `FrozenDict.__hash__` is lazy — does anything actually hash it?

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rnE "hash\(|set\(|frozenset|in \{|dict\[BaselineArtifact|BaselineArtifact:" src/assay_engine/ | grep -i baseline | head

content.text:
The freeze cost is unconditional at construction (it's in `__post_init__`), regardless of whether the artifact is later hashed. The `hash()` per leaf cost is also paid eagerly during the tuple comprehension — let me confirm freeze does call hash per leaf.

Looking at `freeze` (lines 95-96): for each float leaf, after the list/tuple branch recurses down to scalars, it falls through to `hash(value)` at line 96. So yes, every float is individually hashed. This is real pure-Python O(n²) work.

Now let me assess severity properly. The finding is real, but I need to weigh: is this a true production blocker or an architecturally-acceptable cost given the fast path exists?

Key facts:
1. Slow path is real and reproducible (21s/+401MB for 5k matrix).
2. A numpy fast path exists and is ~480x faster with O(1) traversal.
3. **The engine's own primitive `cosine_similarity_matrix` returns `list[list[float]]`**, even on the numpy path (line 112 converts back). So the engine actively steers builders toward the slow representation.
4. No documentation tells builders to use buffer-backed arrays.

This is a genuine performance/scale defect: the engine's own building blocks produce the structure that triggers the pathological path, and there's no guard or guidance.

input.reasoning:
VERIFIED TRUE, with severity recalibrated from HIGH to MEDIUM.

Mechanics confirmed by reading the code:
- BaselineArtifact.__post_init__ (toolkit.py:38-40) routes contents and determinism through freeze_mapping unconditionally at construction.
- freeze() (_frozen.py:61-102) recurses element-by-element through list/tuple (lines 75-76), allocating a parallel nested-tuple copy, and at each scalar leaf calls hash(value) (lines 95-96). For an n*n nested-list payload this is O(n^2) pure-Python work plus an O(n^2) tuple allocation.
- The buffer fast path (lines 85-94) only triggers for objects exposing tobytes()+shape/typecode/dtype (numpy/array.array), canonicalizing to a single (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor in O(1) traversal.

PoC against the venv reproduces the claimed numbers:
- 2000x2000 (4M-cell) nested Python list: 3.6 s, peak +64 MB. (Finding claimed 3.29 s / 64 MB — matches.)
- 5000x5000 (25M-cell) nested list: 21.1 s, peak +401 MB at artifact construction.
- Same 2000x2000 as a numpy array: 0.0075 s, +32 MB (just the single tobytes copy) — ~480x faster and avoids the parallel-tuple allocation entirely.

Crucially, this is not merely a hypothetical "if a builder uses lists" — the engine's OWN baseline primitive cosine_similarity_matrix (primitives.py:115) is typed and implemented to return list[list[float]], and even the vectorized _cosine_matrix_numpy path converts the numpy result back to nested lists before returning (primitives.py:112). So the natural, engine-supplied way to build the most common baseline content (a similarity matrix) produces exactly the structure that hits the pathological freeze path. There is no documentation in toolkit.py or docs/ steering builders toward buffer-backed arrays, and no shallow-immutable container for large arrays.

Why MEDIUM, not HIGH: (a) A working fast path already exists; the defect is a representation/steering gap, not a fundamental impossibility. A builder that stores numpy arrays in contents pays only O(1)-traversal + one tobytes copy. (b) The engine is explicitly self-described as not scaling to thousands of embeddings in pure-Python anyway: cosine_similarity_matrix's own docstring (primitives.py:118) says the pure path "does not scale (a corpus of thousands of embeddings would take minutes-to-hours)" — so the O(n^2*d) similarity computation already dominates before the freeze cost, and at the scales where freeze becomes a problem the upstream matrix build is itself the larger cost. The freeze step adds a second full traversal (~21s for 5k) on top, which is a meaningful regression but secondary to the build itself. (c) It is a deterministic one-time construction cost, not a runtime hot loop or correctness/security issue, so it degrades large studies rather than breaking core functionality. This keeps it above LOW because it is a real, reproducible scale ceiling with the engine's own primitives feeding the slow path, and is fixable cheaply (have cosine_similarity_matrix return a numpy array when numpy is present, and/or document that contents must hold buffer-backed arrays for large payloads).
input.evidence: Read /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py (freeze lines 61-102: list/tuple recursion 75-76, buffer fast path 85-94, per-leaf hash 95-96) and /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/baseline/toolkit.py:38-40 (__post_init__ deep-freeze). Read /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:112,115-131 — cosine_similarity_matrix returns list[list[float]] on BOTH the pure and numpy paths. PoC via /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python: BaselineArtifact freeze of 2000x2000 nested list = 3.6 s / +64 MB; 5000x5000 = 21.1 s / +401 MB; same 2000x2000 numpy array = 0.0075 s / +32 MB (fast path produces ('<f8',(2000,2000),b'...') descriptor). grep of docs/ and toolkit.py found no guidance steering contents toward buffer-backed arrays.

content.text:
I've completed my independent verification.

**Verdict: TRUE finding, severity recalibrated HIGH → MEDIUM.**

The mechanics and PoC numbers in the prior assessor's report are accurate and reproducible against the venv:
- 2000x2000 nested-list freeze: 3.6s / +64MB (matched their 3.29s/64MB)
- 5000x5000: 21.1s / +401MB at artifact construction
- numpy array of same size: 0.0075s (~480x faster, O(1) traversal via the `(kind, shape, bytes)` fast path)

The most important corroborating detail I found that the original report only implied: the engine's **own** baseline primitive `cosine_similarity_matrix` (`src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:115`) is typed and implemented to return `list[list[float]]`, and even its vectorized numpy path converts back to nested lists before returning (line 112). So the natural, engine-supplied way to build the canonical baseline content actively feeds the pathological freeze path, and nothing in `toolkit.py` or `docs/` steers builders toward buffer-backed arrays.

I downgraded to MEDIUM because: a working O(1) fast path already exists (it's a representation/steering gap, not an impossibility); the freeze cost is a one-time deterministic construction cost, not a runtime hot loop or correctness/security issue; and at the scales where freeze hurts, the upstream O(n²·d) similarity build is itself already the documented dominant cost (`cosine_similarity_matrix` docstring admits it "does not scale" to thousands of embeddings). It stays above LOW because it's a real, reproducible scale ceiling fed by the engine's own primitives and cheaply fixable.

Relevant files:
- `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py` (lines 61-102, 75-76, 85-94, 95-96)
- `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/baseline/toolkit.py` (lines 38-40)
- `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py` (lines 112, 115-131)
